Inabita

A bioregional initiative in central Italy's Fiastra Valley, working to strengthen collaborative relationships and shared identity between villages through cultural celebration and participatory planni

Case Study

Bioregional Observatory/4 Bioregional Organizations/Inabita.md
Activities
33314415-10e1-81a2-8a40-fc646c63d0bf, 33314415-10e1-812b-b7c8-df8926e351b0, 33314415-10e1-8133-9a67-cacb70578d60
type
organization
location_lat
43.2
background_section
## Background The Fiastra valley lies in the Sibillini foothills in central Italy—roughly three hours from Rome—across several small municipalities. People describe a shared identity tied tightly to place, threaded through scattered hill villages. Those communities sit in Italy’s “inner areas”: rural territories juggling ageing populations, infrastructure gaps, and economic pressure while trying to keep culture alive. Needs cluster in four strands: **built infrastructure**; **culture**; **environmental education**; and **local production and craft**. ### Origins and Network Since 2010, Ripe San Ginesio has hosted the [biennial Borgofuturo festival](https://www.canva.com/design/DAGc1cZg818/QTw8PNm_Z6GG98iW_aCqBg/edit). In 2020—the tenth edition—young organisers working with the municipality faced COVID-19 lockdowns instead of a usual programme. They used the pause to look beyond one town: mapping needs and possibilities across the whole valley and asking how the festival could respond to both. They confirmed strong place-based identity—and also long-standing rivalries that had limited cooperation.
Sources
33314415-10e1-81d1-a875-c5a87a283652

place
Val di Fiastra, Marche, Italy
activities
Cultural Celebration Across Villages|Participatory Planning and Visioning|Action Plan Implementation
description
A bioregional initiative in central Italy's Fiastra Valley, working to strengthen collaborative relationships and shared identity between villages through cultural celebration and participatory planning. Born from the Borgofuturo festival, the initiative has evolved into "Qui ValdiFiastra"—a 15-project action plan addressing built infrastructure, culture, environmental education, and local production.
id
inabita
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy Inabità remains a cooperative of researchers, planners, architects, and environmental scientists working through integrated, participatory methods. The emphasis is developmental: enabling different stewards to contribute fully while the valley’s own qualities emerge over time. Planning continues with explicit attention to *how* people contribute together—a diversification of roles (likened to an ant colony) so each group works from its strengths. Process and outcome stay paired: bioregional governance is treated as ongoing learning, not a one-off plan.
website
https://www.inabitalab.com
bioregional_context
Nestled in the foothills of the Sibillini mountains in central Italy, the Fiastra valley is a region populated by several rural municipalities facing challenges common to Italy's "inner areas"—depopulation, infrastructure needs, and maintaining cultural identity while building economic resilience. The villages share a strong identity rooted to place but also face long-standing rivalries that have limited collaboration.
